critic_raw:
The primary evaluation metric is human scoring on clarity and informativeness, but the experiment describes using ROUGE-based tuning on the validation set for model selection. This is a direct metric misalignment: the model is selected to maximize ROUGE scores, but success is measured by human judgment. ROUGE optimizes n-gram overlap with reference abstracts; human evaluators assess conceptual clarity and informativeness. These objectives are not interchangeable and are sometimes in opposition — a system that copies specific phrases scores well on ROUGE while a paraphrased but clearer summary may score better with humans. The design's model selection procedure will systematically bias the winning model toward ROUGE rather than the stated primary objective.

Second, 'stratified random split' for academic paper summarization is conceptually strained. The 'stratification variable' is academic domain, but domain is not a label in the classification sense — it is a covariate. More importantly, papers from the same lab, the same conference proceedings, or the same research group will have correlated abstracts and correlated writing styles. A random split will scatter papers from the same authorship cluster across train and test, allowing the model to learn author-specific phrasing that it then 'recognizes' in the test set. A document-level or author/group-level holdout is necessary to test generalization to genuinely new writing styles.

Third, the human evaluation protocol is described vaguely as 'human evaluators, possessing diverse backgrounds and disciplined grounding, will ensure reliability.' This is not a protocol — it is aspirational language. The design does not specify: how many evaluators per summary, how evaluators are calibrated, whether evaluators see the reference abstract (which would contaminate their judgment), inter-annotator agreement measurement, or whether the comparison is blinded to model identity. Without blind paired comparison with measured inter-rater reliability (Krippendorff's alpha or similar), the human evaluation is not reproducible and may reflect evaluator bias toward more verbose or academic-sounding outputs.

Fourth, the comparison to 'traditional extractive summarization' is underspecified. Extractive methods range from simple lead-sentence selection (extremely strong baseline for academic abstracts where the first paragraph often summarizes the paper) to graph-based methods like TextRank. Without specifying the extractive baseline precisely, the comparison cannot distinguish whether hierarchical attention improves over extractive methods in general or only over weak extractive variants.

Fifth, the hypothesis specifically claims improvement in 'relevance' measured by human scores on 'clarity and informativeness' — but the design does not define whether these are evaluated as separate dimensions or combined. Clarity and informativeness can trade off: a clear summary may omit technical detail (lower informativeness), while a technically complete summary may be unclear. Conflating them into a single score obscures the mechanism of any observed improvement.
all_issues_raised: Model selection uses ROUGE-based validation tuning while the primary evaluation metric is human scoring on clarity and informativeness — these objectives conflict, systematically biasing the winning model toward ROUGE optimization rather than the stated human-centered goal.

Stratified random split at the paper level does not account for author/lab-level clustering: papers from the same research group share writing style and vocabulary, so random splits allow the model to learn author-specific patterns that contaminate test-set performance.

Human evaluation protocol is aspirational rather than operational — the design omits evaluator count per summary, blind comparison procedure, whether reference abstracts are visible to raters, and inter-annotator reliability measurement, making results irreproducible.

The extractive summarization baseline is underspecified; the range from lead-sentence selection (a surprisingly strong baseline for structured academic papers) to graph-based TextRank is wide, and the comparison outcome depends critically on which variant is used.

Clarity and informativeness are treated as a combined score but can trade off — a technically complete summary may be less clear, obscuring whether any observed gain is in comprehension or content coverage.
all_issues_adjudicated: Model selection uses ROUGE-based validation tuning while the primary evaluation metric is human scoring on clarity and informativeness — these objectives conflict, systematically biasing the winning model toward ROUGE optimization rather than the stated human-centered goal.

Stratified random split at the paper level does not account for author/lab-level clustering: papers from the same research group share writing style and vocabulary, so random splits allow the model to learn author-specific patterns that contaminate test-set performance.

Human evaluation protocol is aspirational rather than operational — the design omits evaluator count per summary, blind comparison procedure, whether reference abstracts are visible to raters, and inter-annotator reliability measurement, making results irreproducible.

The extractive summarization baseline is underspecified; the range from lead-sentence selection (a surprisingly strong baseline for structured academic papers) to graph-based TextRank is wide, and the comparison outcome depends critically on which variant is used.

Clarity and informativeness are treated as a combined score but can trade off — a technically complete summary may be less clear, obscuring whether any observed gain is in comprehension or content coverage.
